content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5662         322 5660              322 5901        • • • 322 6441         322 6444

December 14, 1979

Gerald A. Sperry
City Attorney
City of Stockton
425 North El Dorado Street
Stockton, CA 95202

A 99-098

Dear Mr. Sperry:

In your letter of October 31, 1979, you raise several
questions concerning the impact of the Political Reform Act
of 1974 ("the Act") upon activities and interests of newly
elected Stockton City Councilmember Barbara Fass. Ms. Fass
is a partner in a small law firm. Ms. Fass, as well as
other members of the firm, occasionally represent clients
whose interests are adverse to the city.

Your questions generally go to what Councilmember Fass
may do in her capacity as a lawyer. Questions about the
propriety of representing a client as an attorney are primarily governed by the Rules of Professional Conduct of the
State Bar of California. See, e.g., People v. Rhodes, 13
Cal. 3d 180 (1974). The primary concern of the Act is with
what a public official does in his or her official capacity,
and our answer to your specific questions must therefore
focus on Councilmember Fass' official duties rather than
upon her private acts.

The Political Reform Act's general prohibition against
conflicts of interest is contained in Government Code Section
87100 which provides that:

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

A decision is one in which Ms. Fass has a financial
interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision

Gerald A. Sperry
December 14, 1979
Page Two


will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on any business entity
in which she has an investment or is a partner or employee
or on any other source of income to her of more than $250,
including sources of income to a business entity of which
she owns 10 percent or more if her pro rata share of income
to the entity is more than $250. See Government Code Sections
82030 and 87103; for a definition of "material financial
effect" see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (copy enclosed).

In addition, our regulations provide that an official
uses his or her official position to influence a decision
when he or she furthers or attempts to affect in any manner
any decision within or before his or her agency or before
any agency which is appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(e). Therefore, particular actions taken by Ms. Pass
as an attorney in representing the best interests of a client
may also be attempts to use her official position to influence
a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act. For example, if it is necessary for Ms. Pass to
contact any city official appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the City Council with the intent to
persuade, negotiate with or otherwise attempt to influence
that official to commit his or her agency to any course of
action, Ms. Pass would be attempting to use her official
position to influence a governmental decision. If it is
reasonably foreseeable such a decision would have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on Ms. Pass' client, her actions would
violate Government Code Section 87100.

Because the conflict of interest provisions of the Act
restrict the actions only of public officials, it does not
limit the representational activities of other members of
employees of her law firm. As mentioned above, however, if
her pro rata share of fees the client pays to the firm exceed
$250, the client is a source of income to her, and she cannot
participate in any of the City Council's decisions which
will have a material financial effect on the client.

Finally, you ask whether Councilmember Pass or her law
firm may represent a criminal defendant where the veracity
of one or more city police officers is directly attacked.
By telephone you have conveyed Ms. Pass' concern that it may
be desirable for defense counsel in such a case to attempt
to impeach the police officer by compelling the production
of civilian complaints filed in the officer's personnel
file.

Gerald A. Sperry
December 14, 1979
Page Three


     Any effort directed to the court by Ms. Fass to compel
by judicial process alone the production of evidence would
not constitute the making of, participation in the making of
or attempting to use official position to influence a gov-
ernmental decision within the meaning of Government Code
Section 87100. Consequently, representation of a criminal
defendant in the manner you have described would not violate
the Act. Other requirements of law may, however, be relevant.
You may wish to consult Government Code Section 1126 and
People v. Municipal Court, 138 Cal. Rptr. 235 (App. 1977).

     If I can be of further assistance, please call.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Deputy Chief for
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Legal Division

STC:plh
Enclosure

79998

CITY OF STOCKTON

OFFICE
OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
STOCKTON, CA 95202


October 31, 1979


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

ATTENTION:  Conflict of Interest Division

RE:  POSSIBLE CONFLICT OF INTEREST WHEN ATTORNEY-COUNCILPERSON (BARBARA FASS) REPRESENTS CLIENTS IN CASES ADVERSE TO THE CITY

This inquiry is on behalf of newly-elected Councilperson
Barbara Fass.  In Stockton, members of the City Council are
paid only $15.00 per meeting and thus retain outside employment.
Ms. Fass is a partner in a small law firm which occasionally represents clients whose interests are adverse to
the City.  For example, currently she is the attorney of record
in a suit against the City of Stockton seeking to overturn the
condemnation of the Fox Theater in downtown Stockton.

It is the view of this office that merely stepping down when
matters involving Councilperson Fass's law firm come before
the Council does not solve the conflict of interest problems.
Ms. Fass will have access to information concerning City
litigation which may be helpful to one or more of her firm's
clients.  Further, not participating (stepping down) in a
Council vote is the equivalent to a "No" vote on the matter.

Therefore, Ms. Fass would appreciate your opinion as to these
situations generally, and specifically:

1.  May Councilperson Fass or her law firm represent a
client having interests adverse to the City, including defendants
in violation of City ordinances?


'Stockton...Someplace Special!'

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 31, 1979
Page -2-


     2.  May Councilperson Fass or her firm represent a criminal
defendant where the veracity of one or more Stockton police
officers is directly attacked?

     3.  May Councilperson Fass or her firm represent before
the City Council a client whose interests are adverse to the
City?

Since Councilperson Fass takes her seat on the Council next
week, your early response will be deeply appreciated.


[signature]

GERALD A. SPERRY
CITY ATTORNEY

GAS:JKG:plc

cc:  Councilperson Barbara Fass
